title: Upgrades Boost Database Market (data mining and SGI-IBM rivalry)
author: John Foley
abstract: InformationWeek (Issue 575) reporting Silicon Graphics' data-mining entry against IBM at DB Expo, San Francisco. Context: Sybase and Informix warned on Q1 1996 revenue, triggering double-digit share-price declines (Informix -28% in one day). Kastner (Aberdeen Group analyst) calls the sell-off 'a wrong conclusion' from individual situations — argues database leaders remain solid double-digit growers. Oracle then reassured analysts, sparking database-stock rebound.
importance_rationale: Contemporaneous record of mid-1990s database-industry inflection: Informix guidance shock, DB Expo '96 SGI/IBM data-mining rivalry, and Kastner's contrarian defense of database fundamentals.
relevance_rationale: Useful for understanding why Oracle won (and Informix lost) the 1990s RDBMS consolidation; data-mining origins relevant to modern analytics and AI.
prescience_rationale: Kastner's 'these are all good companies and they will continue to grow at robust double-digit rates' was accurate for Oracle (massive winner) but wrong for Sybase (fading to niche, acquired by SAP 2010) and Informix (acquired by IBM 2001 after continued decline). Net: mostly wrong on individual companies, right on the category.
